text:
... although it gets muddy where you draw the line between "code" and "logic", which is part of the design of software. You have to be able to specify what the software is and what it does. If "code" is that stuff you feed to a compiler, then there's no fundamental reason why the specification has to be in the form of "code" in that sense. But if "code" means a specification of data and behaviors, well then it's all the same thing. But at that rate, any tool or user interface constitutes a "language", and whatever model or info you put into it constitutes "code".

Personally, I agree that it's all code, and that "no code" just means, not what people traditionally associate with as being "code" ("none of _that_ stuff")

text:
I just joined this group but I think this could be an interesting discussion from <https://www.indiehackers.com/post/the-api-business-90020c642b?utm_campaign=digest-daily&utm_medium=email&utm_source=indie-hackers-emails|Indie Hackers> (not written be me):

“I’ve been thinking recently about API businesses. This movement seems to have started with some companies many Indie Hackers will know well, such as Stripe & Twilio. I’m seeing it more and more:

• <https://plaid.com/uk/|Plaid> - API for open banking
• <https://duffel.com/|Duffell> - API for travel
• <https://payitoff.io/|Pay It Off> - API for student loads
• <https://alpaca.markets/|Alpaca> - API for open banking
I wanted to understand what makes these markets suitable for APIs as a business. Thinking out loud here...
1. There seems to be a bias towards finance/fintech
2. The underlying supplier seems large, slow, complicated incumbents (banks, airlines, etc)
3. Some of these are regulated industries
Any other things you noticed about these API companies? What makes them good businesses? Has anyone else seen any other interesting API businesses?”

text: Also I dont think this discussion is  for this group - maybe in <#C5U3SEW6A|random>, but this discussion is more for a “entrepeneur” group. API businesses have been around for years on end, it’s not a movement. And the only common thing among all of them is that they’re a layer that does something - wether it be getting weather or sending nukes, they are just a layer that does a task. Even calling them all API businesses is wrong - some are API businesses - where invoking a command does something, others are only database like, some are just middleware and proxy connecting the dots and their business is more sales than API.

text:
<@U01176YGU4W> I can speak on API for banking/paying/subscriptions — I built a company around the time Stripe came out (more or less doing the same thing) but did not take it to market as we used it internally in another company. The complexities inside banking/ACH/creditcards is insane. Honestly, it’s the definition of insanity. Banks and cards are very difficult to work with because their tech is absolute shit. So APIs that make that easy… very valuable.  (Happy to share more of my experience there, just DM me)

I don’t think the finance/fintech note is exclusive or even a bias. It’s more business as a whole. Consumers rather use interfaces, so I would be comfortable drawing the line of a bias towards businesses. API tools are all about ease of use: providing a better UX than dealing with the terrible tech underneath it. Think of API’s as more access to information than a programming interface. If you are intereseted in building an API as a business I have a friend, Travis, that would love to chat more building the company Saasify.sh who has a lot of experience in this topic.

text: If you want to build an API as a business: don’t start there. A business is something that solves a problem: API is an implementation detail. You need to find the problem before applying API as a solution.

elements.text: If you want to build an API as a business: don’t start there. A business is something that solves a problem: API is an implementation detail. You need to find the problem before applying API as a solution.

text: :clap: <@UAVCC2X70> Code is a means to the end. We need to keep focus on the business-logic. What is it that you are building/fixing and how do we build/fix it in the fastest and most sustainable way. The future of coding is more about the people than the code. How can we get more people into this ecosystem? What tools will they need? What problems are they solving? How do they interact with computer? We already have amazing tech, it’s time to bring it to the the other 99%.

text: "Where do people spend the most of their time in writing software?" In my job, seems like (1) reading code (including 3rd-party stuff with poor documentation), (2) debugging (including reading StackOverflow about error messages) (3) thinking about how to solve problems under the constraint of minimizing "risk" and changes to the zillion lines of code that are already written, (4) writing code to solve the problem, (5) refactoring code, (6) being distracted by more interesting things on the internet, (7) writing test code, (8) commit management - reviewing, writing commit messages, merging, rebasing, fiddling with task board, etc., (9) scrums/meetings, (10) searching for & reading documentation. (Disclaimer: just guessing, and reading code only comes first in low-quality codebases)

text:
Is there a good modern example of a structured editor?

My main worry about structured editing is that a naive implementation raises the floor but lowers the ceiling, in the sense that I'd be slower in a structured editor than in a regular editor with a language I know well. I'm especially interested in examples that disprove that (I believe it's possible, I just think it would take someone smarter than me to do it :))
